newsitems.title: Development Update #2
newsitems.author: Robyn
newsitems.contents: Welcome to Development Update #2!Welcome to Development Update #2!Looking back over May, it feels like a bit of a blur because so much work has been done.The main focus this month ended up being a hard look at some of the core mechanics and making some fairly significant changes to the game. Those decisions are never easy when you’ve already invested months of work into something, but I’m through that process now and I genuinely think the game is much better because of it.Before I get into the meat of the changes, one small update going forward.Patreon supporters will continue to get early access to these development updates, but after a couple of weeks I’ll also make them publicly available so that people outside of Patreon can follow the development of LIVING HELL as well.As always, thank you for supporting the project.OverviewAs I’ve mentioned previously, I have a publisher for the game: Future Friends.One of the biggest advantages of having a publisher involved this early is that I can get people to playtest the game internally and give very honest feedback without worrying about anything leaking. They’re invested in helping the game succeed, so I get a lot of useful criticism from them and that’s incredibly valuable at this stage of development.From the beginning I’ve always told them that I want the most honest feedback possible. Praise is nice to hear, of course, but it doesn’t help much when you’re trying to improve a game. What’s useful is finding the things that aren’t working and figuring out why.One thing I’ve learned over the years, especially from working on DEVOUR, is that when somebody gives negative feedback it’s usually worth paying attention to. That doesn’t mean they’re always right about the solution, but they’re often right that there’s a problem somewhere. I believe everyone’s perspective is valid.It’s very easy as a developer to apply band-aids. If players get lost, you can add a map. If they don’t know what to do, you can throw a giant tutorial on the screen. If they don’t know where to go, you can add objective markers everywhere. Those things can work, but I always try to step back and ask why the player got confused in the first place.Usually there’s a deeper issue underneath and if you can solve that instead, the whole game becomes stronger.I’ve probably gone through this process a dozen times already during the development of LIVING HELL. Every time it happens it means revisiting systems I’ve already spent weeks or months working on, which is never particularly fun. It costs time, money and sleep! But every single time I’ve gone through that process, the game has come out better on the other side.The biggest piece of feedback I was seeing, both from publisher playtests and from sessions with friends, was that there was too much friction in certain parts of the experience.LIVING HELL takes place in a large city, and one of the dangers when designing a large environment is giving players too much freedom. In an open-world game that’s often a good thing. In a game where you’re racing against sunrise and every minute matters, it’s important to be respectful of the player’s time.Players generally understood the overall objective, but there were moments where they weren’t completely sure where they should be heading or what they should be focusing on next. Sometimes they would spend longer navigating than I’d like, and sometimes the path forward wasn’t as clear as it should have been.That became the focus of most of my work throughout May. I’ll split it up into 3 different topics.1. Reworking the TerminalThe first major thing I looked at was the artificial intelligence system and terminal interface.If you’ve seen the trailer on the Steam page, you’ve probably seen the terminal already. In the trailer it actually comes across really well, and I still like a lot of what it brought to the atmosphere and lore of the game.The problem was that over time I had gradually started using it for more and more things.It was handling menu systems, travel selection, player guidance, objectives, lore, tutorials and various other interactions. The more playtests I watched, the more I noticed a fairly simple truth that I’ve always known: people don’t really like reading in co-op games.Players are talking to their friends, exploring, reacting to what’s happening around them and trying to keep the pace moving. Even in single-player games you have to be careful about how much reading you ask people to do these days.A lot of work had gone into the terminal system, so it wasn’t an easy decision, but I ultimately felt it was worth stepping back and rethinking how that information was being presented.One thing that was important to me was preserving the lore. I still wanted players to feel connected to UREI (United Recovery of Earth Initiative) and the wider story of Pelican City. I didn’t want to remove those elements. I just wanted to present them in a way that felt more natural and immersive.Without givin...

newsitems.title: Behind the scenes of LIVING HELL!
newsitems.author: Olima
newsitems.contents: Hi, hope you are having a good start to the year!Pleased to say I’m making great progress on LIVING HELL. It feels like the game is starting to come together, which is super exciting!! I know a lot of you want to see more, but there’s still some core features I need to lock in before we can show stuff to the public in detail. Thank you all for your patience 🙏 That being said, there are a few things I can show off. Here’s a look at the models of some playable characters, as well as the houses and buildings that make up Pelican City.Playable characters and monsters A few months ago we shared an extended trailer that ended with a sneak peek at the six playable characters. Each of them will have different passives, but we’ve also added small details to the models that give you hints about the world of LIVING HELL. In these designs, I’ve gone for a mix of old-school and modern horror. Each model has lip-sync animations, and you will see mutation effects from being exposed to the sun’s radiation.The real stars of LIVING HELL are, of course, the mutated creatures that you encounter in the game. I’m working with a super talented artist who helped me bring them to life. While a lot of the horror will come from how they move and behave, I’m already proud of how the models turned out. They’re pretty terrifying just spinning around…. The creatures were created by a Japanese artist called ShineiRoku, who is deservedly well known online for their incredible, unsettling work. I feel really lucky that we got to collaborate and would recommend checking out more of their stuff! A look at the world of LIVING HELLHere’s some new screenshots of the streets of Pelican City. It’s seen better days, as you might imagine. The killer sun changes the light and shadow dramatically as it moves through the sky. The buildings themselves were created in partnership by another super talented artist called Neostar Games. Bear in mind these are renders and don’t represent how it would look in moment-to-moment gameplay, but hopefully should give you some idea of the architecture and atmosphere within the world! That’s all we can reveal for now, but stay tuned for more updates soon! All the best, Joe☀️

newsitems.title: End of Year Update and Publisher News!
newsitems.author: Fenda
newsitems.contents: As we head into the new year, I just wanted to share a quick update on LIVING HELL and what’s coming up in 2026. First off, thanks so much to everyone who’s wishlisted the game or shared kind words since the announcement in September. Announcing a new game is always nerve-wracking but the reaction to the trailer and the idea behind LIVING HELL has been genuinely brilliant, and we already have over 40,000 Wishlists, so thank you! It’s easy to second-guess yourself when you’re deep in development, but seeing people excited about the game gave me a massive lift. I really do think this will be my best horror game yet. I’m happy to say that development has been going extremely well since the announcement. There’s still a way to go, but I’ve been making very good progress and working on a lot of small details I’m really proud of and can’t wait to show you. Now for a bit of news I’ve been looking forward to sharing: I’ve decided to team up with a publisher! We handled all the marketing for DEVOUR ourselves, which was great, but this time around, I wanted to focus fully on the game itself and find a team that could help with the rest. The publisher I’ve partnered with is called Future Friends Games. They’re a smaller team, but they genuinely care about indie games and play them themselves, which was really important to me. They’ve published great games like CloverPit and The Cabin Factory, and also helped on games like Vampire Survivors and Sledding Game that had strong multiplayer modes. I think they get what LIVING HELL is going for, and I’m excited to have them on board. With their support, I should be able to share more regular updates throughout next year, including behind-the-scenes content, development updates, and eventually opportunities to playtest and provide feedback on early builds. If that’s something you’re into, keep an eye out for upcoming Steam news posts! That’s it from me for now. Thanks again for being here. Wishing you all a great holiday, a good start to the new year, and I can’t wait to share more with you soon. Joe
